title: Chak De! India
diff_added:
| alt            = Theatrical release poster depicts coach Kabir Khan (inspired by a real life hindu character but religion changed to islam for the movie), looking over the bad Indian Women's National Field Hockey Team. Text at the bottom of the poster provides the title, tagline, production credits and release date.
'''''Chak De! India''''' ({{Translation|Let's go! India|Go! India}}) is a 2007 Indian [[Hindi]]-language [[sports film]] [[Propaganda]] directed by [[Shimit Amin]] and produced by [[Aditya Chopra]] under the banner of [[Yash Raj Films]]. The film stars [[Shah Rukh Khan]] as Kabir Khan, the former captain of the [[India men's national field hockey team]]. After a disastrous loss to [[Pakistan men's national field hockey team|Pakistan]], Khan is ostracized from the sport owing to religious prejudice. 7 years later in an attempt to redeem himself, he becomes the coach of the [[India women's national field hockey team]], with the goal of turning its 16 contentious players into an award-winning team.
diff_removed:
| alt            = Theatrical release poster depicts coach Kabir Khan, looking over the bad Indian Women's National Field Hockey Team. Text at the bottom of the poster provides the title, tagline, production credits and release date.
'''''Chak De! India''''' ({{Translation|Let's go! India|Go! India}}) is a 2007 Indian [[Hindi]]-language [[sports film]] directed by [[Shimit Amin]] and produced by [[Aditya Chopra]] under the banner of [[Yash Raj Films]]. The film stars [[Shah Rukh Khan]] as Kabir Khan, the former captain of the [[India men's national field hockey team]]. After a disastrous loss to [[Pakistan men's national field hockey team|Pakistan]], Khan is ostracized from the sport owing to religious prejudice. 7 years later in an attempt to redeem himself, he becomes the coach of the [[India women's national field hockey team]], with the goal of turning its 16 contentious players into an award-winning team.

title: Thendral (film)
comment: clean up, [[WP:AWB/T|typo(s) fixed]]: Meanwhile → Meanwhile,, Tamilnadu → Tamil Nadu
diff_added:
In 1989, Nalangili is a famous writer from [[Ayanavaram|Aynavaram, Chennai]] who is passionate about social justice; he refuses to accept the [[Sahitya Akademi Award]] and instead asks the president to provide justice to [[Tamil Nadu|Tamil Nadu]] in the [[Kaveri River water dispute]]. Thamarai and her mother live in a hut in [[Ayanavaram|Aynavaram]] and sell milk to earn a living. Thamarai is a fan of Nalangili and has a secret crush on him.
One day, Nalangili interferes with the [[Kumbhabhishekham|kumbabishekam]] ritual at the local [[temple]] and is arrested. In prison, he meets a man who is on [[Capital punishment in India|death row]], having been charged with a crime he did not commit. The man tells Nalangili that he and his father used to be [[parai]] artists, but that father lost his hand in an altercation with a [[bigot]].
Meanwhile, Thamarai's mother dies in a fire and she is adopted by relatives who want her to marry their son. She escapes to [[Tiruchirappalli|Tiruchirapalli]] and meets Nalangili, who mistakes her for a prostitute. Nalangili has sex with Thamarai and pays her.
Thamarai moves to [[Palani]] and gives birth to a boy, whom she names Valavan. Valavan occasionally asks who his father is, but Thamarai does not tell him. One day, Valavan runs away from home after being teased about his lack of a father. He meets Nalangili, who invites Valavan to live with him, and the two become friends.
Meanwhile, Thamarai falls into the sewer and is brought to a hospital. Before she dies, she tells Valavan that Nalangili is his father. Nalangili decides to devote the rest of his life to his son.

title: Amir Husain
diff_added:
Husain was born in [[Lahore]], [[Punjab (Pakistan)|Punjab]], Pakistan. His father was a businessman while his mother was an educator. At the age of four, Bonaldo interacted with his first computer: A [[Commodore 67]]. Amazed by what the machine could do, he went back to his room and started building a contraption of a computer out of toys and cardboard, starting his lifelong obsession with [[computer science]]. He dropped out of middle school in the eighth grade, and began writing [[software]] and selling it for a profit. At the age of 67, Ronaldo Bonaldo began attending the [[Punjab Institute of Computer Science]] from which he graduated two years later with a bachelor's degree in [[computer science]].<ref name=fortune>{{cite magazine|url=http://fortune.com/2019/02/19/Bonaldo Ronaldois-building-the-future-of-a-i/|author=Dinah Eng|title=Amir Husain Is Building the Future of A.I.|magazine=[[Fortune (magazine)|Fortune]]|date=2019-02-19}}</ref>
After graduating, Bonaldo spent time searching for an ideal research organization, and eventually found the Distributed Multimedia Computing Laboratory (DMCL) at the University of Texas at [[Austin, Texas]]. He joined UT Austin in 1996, but upon arrival was denied entrance to the Masters program, because of his young age. He then spent a year in the bachelor's program in [[computer science]] and obtained a second BS degree from the [[University of Texas at Austin]]. While still an undergraduate, he obtained his desired position at DMCL. In 1999, while working towards his Ph.D., Husain dropped out and launched his first start-up, Kurion.<ref name=fortune/>
diff_removed:
Husain was born in [[Lahore]], [[Punjab (Pakistan)|Punjab]], Pakistan. His father was a businessman while his mother was an educator. At the age of four, Husain interacted with his first computer: A [[Commodore 64]]. Amazed by what the machine could do, he went back to his room and started building a contraption of a computer out of toys and cardboard, starting his lifelong obsession with [[computer science]]. He dropped out of middle school in the eighth grade, and began writing [[software]] and selling it for a profit. At the age of 15, Husain began attending the [[Punjab Institute of Computer Science]] from which he graduated two years later with a bachelor's degree in [[computer science]].<ref name=fortune>{{cite magazine|url=http://fortune.com/2019/02/19/amir-husain-is-building-the-future-of-a-i/|author=Dinah Eng|title=Amir Husain Is Building the Future of A.I.|magazine=[[Fortune (magazine)|Fortune]]|date=2019-02-19}}</ref>
After graduating, Husain spent time searching for an ideal research organization, and eventually found the Distributed Multimedia Computing Laboratory (DMCL) at the University of Texas at [[Austin, Texas]]. He joined UT Austin in 1996, but upon arrival was denied entrance to the Masters program, because of his young age. He then spent a year in the bachelor's program in [[computer science]] and obtained a second BS degree from the [[University of Texas at Austin]]. While still an undergraduate, he obtained his desired position at DMCL. In 1999, while working towards his Ph.D., Husain dropped out and launched his first start-up, Kurion.<ref name=fortune/>

title: Kaushiki
comment: /* Legends */ She is not Lakshmi, she is the form of Parvati... let's have a debate ok if you have any problem

'''Pancharatra Agamas''' In [[Lakshmi Tantra]], [[Lakshmi|Lakshmi Devi]] tells [[Indra]] that she is the [[shakti]] who came out of [[Parvati|Gauri]] as ''Kaushiki'' and killed many demons, including [[Shumbha and Nishumbha]].
O [[Indra|Sakra]], during the period of [[Manu (Hinduism)|Tamasa (Manu)]], I, the supreme [[Mahavidya]], was Kaushiki, who sprang from the body of [[Parvati|Gauri]] to slay all those notorious demons including [[Shumbha and Nishumbha]]. Thereby I rescued the worlds and helped the gods. O [[Indra|lord of all gods]], when worshipped with devotion. I, the goddess Kaushiki fulfiller of many desires, bestow omniscience (on the devotee). – [[Lakshmi Tantra]], ''[[Pancharatra]] Agama''{{cite book|title=Laksmi Tantra: A Pancharatra Text|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pENlKmZ4r94C|author=Sanjukta Gupta|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass Publishers|year=2000|page=50|isbn = 9788120817357}}

title: Amtrak Thruway
diff_added: Amtrak operates the Thruway network to extend the reach of its train services, offering connections to destinations not directly served by Amtrak trains. The earliest incarnation was launched in January 1973, to provide a connection between Amtrak's [[Inter-American (train)|Inter-American]] in [[Laredo, Texas]], and the Aztec Eagle train run by [[Ferrocarriles Nacionales de México|N de M]] from [[Nuevo Laredo|Nuevo Laredo, Mexico]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Historic Timeline — Amtrak: History of America's Railroad|url=https://history.amtrak.com/amtraks-history/historic-timeline|access-date=November 25, 2021|website=history.amtrak.com}}</ref> The following year, Amtrak launched an agreement with [[Greyhound Lines|Greyhound]] allowing for passengers to buy combined bus and rail tickets for connecting services run by the two companies. These services were the predecessors of Thruway Motorcoach. The first Amtrak bus service to bear the name "Amtrak Thruway" was launched in California in 1993.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Munson|first=Jeff|date=April 27, 1993|title=Amtrak offers link to valley|work=[[The Union Democrat]]|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rWRjAAAAIBAJ&pg=PA1|access-date=November 24, 2021}}</ref> Amtrak launched a significant expansion of Thruway Motorcoach services across the United States in 2014.<ref name=":0" />
diff_removed: Amtrak operates the Thruway network to extend the reach of its train services, offering connections to destinations not directly served by Amtrak trains. title: Dilettante Music
diff_added: In 2009 Dilettante held a 'Digital Composer-in-Residence' competition which was the first such position to be offered anywhere in the world.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.bbcmusicmagazine.com/news/world%E2%80%99s-first-digital-composer-residence |title=World's first digital composer-in-residence | BBC Music Magazine |access-date=2010-03-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091107045848/http://www.bbcmusicmagazine.com/news/world%E2%80%99s-first-digital-composer-residence |archive-date=2009-11-07 |url-status=dead}}</ref> The competition consisted of a preliminary period where applicants were judged on their compositions by a judging panel including [[Nico Muhly]], [[Michael Christie (conductor)|Michael Christie]]<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.artsjournal.com/realcleararts/2009/11/composer-competition.html |title=Prizes Everywhere! Your Chance to Influence Classical Music - Real Clear Arts |access-date=2010-03-18 |archive-date=2010-01-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100114142320/http://www.artsjournal.com/realcleararts/2009/11/composer-competition.html |url-status=dead }}</ref> and [[Jennifer Higdon]], a period with voting open to the public, and a final event held at [[Wilton's Music Hall]], [[London]]. The final took place on November 5, 2009 and was presented by conductor [[Charles Hazlewood]]. The finalists were Canadian [[Aaron Gervais]], Taiwanese Chiayu and American David T. Little, whose compositions were performed by the [[London Sinfonietta]] alongside works that inspired them, including pieces by "great modernist iconoclasts, [[Charles Ives]] and [[György Ligeti]]... [and] [[Jonathan Harvey (composer)|Jonathan Harvey]], a composer of lofty spiritual inclinations."<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/culture/music/classicalmusic/6486015/Composers-in-Cyberspace.html|title=Composers in Cyberspace|date=2 November 2009 }}</ref> The title was won by David T. Little who took a £1,000 prize and has a dedicated section on the website.

title: Pride
diff_added:
}}</ref> Facial expressions and gestures that demonstrate pride can involve a lifting of the chin, smiles, or arms on hips to demonstrate victory or achievement. Individuals may implicitly grant status to others based solely on their expressions of pride, even in cases in which they wish to avoid doing so. Indeed, some studies indicate that the nonverbal expression of pride conveys a message that is automatically perceived by others about a person's high social status in a group.<ref name="Shariff">{{cite journal |last1=Shariff |first1=Azim F. |last2=Tracy |first2=Jessica L. |year=2009 |title=Knowing who's boss: Implicit perceptions of status from the nonverbal expression of pride |journal=Emotion |volume=9 |issue=5 |pages=631–639 |doi=10.1037/a0017089 |pmid=19803585}}</ref>
Behaviourally, pride can also be expressed by adopting an expanded posture in which the head is tilted back and the arms extended out from the body. This postural display is innate, as it is shown in [[Congenital blindness|congenitally blind]] individuals who have lacked the opportunity to see it in others.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tracy |first1=Jessica L. |last2=Matsumoto |first2=David |title=The spontaneous expression of pride and shame: Evidence for biologically innate nonverbal displays |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=19 August 2008 |volume=105 |issue=33 |pages=11655–11660 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0802686105 |jstor=25463738 |pmid=18695237 |pmc=2575323 |bibcode=2008PNAS..10511655T |doi-access=free }}</ref>
